children.text: The snares suggest connections to a long history of pistas and mixtaps which preceded the breakthrough pop smash and which remain as audible, palpable, if subtle remnants of an unbridled, underground, sample-based past in a genre that has since embraced slick synthesizers and commercial channels

Reggaetón is widely recognized as a Puerto Rican musical innovation that emerged from the underground and nightclub scenes of San Juan in the 1990s. At its core, the genre is a fusion of dancehall and rap, often described as hip hop lyricism performed over dancehall-derived rhythmic structures. Yet this formulation only captures part of the story. Reggaetón developed through a broader trans-Caribbean and diasporic musical ecology, shaped by the simultaneous circulation and popularization of Jamaican dancehall, Afro-Panamanian
, and Latin hip-hop in New York City, as well as the cultural interfaces among these sites.

These genres did not evolve in isolation. In fact, throughout the late twentieth century, dancehall and hip-hop frequently traveled together, heard across the Caribbean and its diasporas as complementary “Black musics” that shared aesthetic, social, and political affinities. In Panama,

emerged through a direct adoption of Jamaican musical traditions, resulting in a localized practice that closely emulated dancehall’s sonic and performative conventions. In contrast, Puerto Rico’s engagement with these sounds was more indirect, mediated in part through New York City, where Caribbean and African American communities lived in proximity and were often racialized within a shared framework of Blackness.

New York thus played a pivotal role in reggaetón’s formation. Within its dense, multiethnic neighborhoods, Panamanians, Jamaicans, Puerto Ricans, Dominicans, and African Americans encountered one another through everyday social life and through music. Genres like reggae and hip-hop resonated powerfully in these contexts as expressive forms capable of articulating emergent, diasporic Black subjectivities and solidarities. It was through this urban contact zone that dancehall circulated into Puerto Rico—not directly from Kingston, but refracted through the aesthetics, technologies, and cultural politics of New York hip hop.
As these sounds took root in San Juan, aspiring rappers began to transition toward dancehall-inspired production, framing their performances as rap delivered over

that resonated more closely with Caribbean rhythmic sensibilities. This shift was not merely musical, but semiotic: dancehall’s association with transnational Blackness, urban modernity, and countercultural resistance offered a powerful framework through which artists could position themselves. Indeed, reggaetón’s early uptake in Puerto Rico was deeply tied to these social meanings.  Its sounds, styles, and performance practices indexed forms of marginality, resistance, and urban belonging that were both locally grounded and globally intelligible. Although reggaetón has since become a global commercial phenomenon, these racialized sensibilities continue to echo in the sonic and stylistic templates that structure its contemporary production.
The following sections provide a guided, audio history of the development of reggaetón from its sample-based origins.

The predominant rhythmic orientation of reggaetón is derived directly from dancehall reggae. While there is also influence from roots reggae, dancehall's textures, tempos, rapid-fire flow, and minimalist focus on 3+3+2 cross rhythms demarcated by heavy, synthesized drums have had a stronger stylistic influence. One of the most enduring features of this influence still audible in reggaetón today is the following
, a 4/4 kick-drum pulse with syncopated snares popularized in Shabba Ranks' song "Dem Bow" (1990).

children.caption: Skeleton of the Dem Bow Riddim from Marshall (2009)

children.text: With the growth and influence of New York's Jamaican community in the 1980s, by the 1990s dancehall had worked its way into the sets of hip-hop DJs and became a culturally charged feature of the New York soundscape. These riddims, such as the examples below, would later be incorporated into the underground recordings that laid the musical foundations for reggaetón in San Juan.
While dancehall reggae gave proto-reggaetón its musical structure, the

of the early 1990s also drew an equal amount of stylistic influences from gangster rap and the "Latin rap". Luis Armando Lozado Cruz —known professionally as Vico C— is a foundational figure in this development. As the first artist to show how one could rap entirely and compellingly in Spanish, Vico C is often recognized as the father of

While reggaetón drew from dancehall musically, the flow (i.e., scripting and delivery) of the lyrics and performance of artistic personae drew more heavily from rap and hip-hop, so much that the genre is often described as hip-hop flow layered on top of a reggae beat.

Since the 1980s, dancehall reggae has dominated the Panamanian popular music scene, in particular the works of artists like El General, Nando Boom, and Chicho Man who produced Spanish-language cover versions of contemporary dancehall hits over the original Jamaican
These remakes played a key role in shaping the dancehall scene in Puerto Rico and the Spanish-language dancehall scene in New York. Particularly influential is the Columbia Records production
(1991, prod. Karl Miller), which provided a template for layering Spanish-language lyrics over Jamaican riddims.
, the Puerto Rican producer Jorge Oquendo alongside Vico C released the 8-track EP

(1991), a calculated attempt to develop a Latin pop/rap hybrid by bringing rising Puerto Rican rappers together with established merengue musicians. The track "Te Ves Buena Remix," —a sample-based, hip-hop-inflected remix of El General's dancehall track by the same name— is perhaps the closest track on this project to sound like the productions soon to emerge from the parallel, grassroots development of rap-reggae fusions in San Juan.

found their way to San Juan via Jorge Oquendo and DJ Negro, though these productions were quickly supplanted by a similar sort of hip-hop dancehall fusion produced by DJ Negro and DJ Playero. This new hybrid drew from reggae's "riddim method" and hip-hop's practices of sampling to merge the familiar melodic contours of dancehall and hip-hop with lyrics performed by emerging rappers like Baby Rasta, Daddy Yankee, Gringo, and Ivy Queen that could not be clearly classified as either dancehall or rap. This new genre—called

abstracted Shabba Rank's "dem bow" riddim to become an important signifier of artistic affiliation, one that to this day is still repurposed and combined with other elements to create distinctiveness within a structured sonic community.

content.caption: Basic Elements of Puerto Rico's localized Dem Bow

children.text: These underground recordings literally circulated outside mainstream Puerto Rican commercial channels as cassette tapes made from a small run of master tapes until about 1994. This underground distribution was in part due to the music's inherited reputation as the obscene music of the (racialized) underclass and clashes with the National Center on Sexual Exploitation (NCOSE, formerly Morality in Media) to keep them out of mainstream channels. It wasn't until 1999 when DJ Nelson and DJ Coyote managed to get San Juan's mix 107.7 fm to begin a 24/7 reggaetón format station, and the genre began to expand into the ears of the mainstream.

Over the late 1990s and early 2000s, underground became less underground both in terms of aesthetics and economics. Around this time, artists and producers (namely DJ Nelson and Daddy Yankee) began to call the genre "
", accordingly inspired as a simplification of "

" or an augmentative form of reggae, roughly translating to "big reggae". The popularity of these recordings is in part responsible for the conventionalization of this new name with this music, in particular the synthesized, new beats that, aside from the dembow template, did not cleanly switch into sampled hip-hop and reggae beats as cleanly.

Thanks to technological developments in tools for producing and distributing recordings, reggaetón was no longer bound by Puerto Rico's shores or even the broader diasporic circuits in New York. Artists began to mix the dancehall inspired riddims like the dembow template with the propulsive sounds of techno/tecno and later the "tropical" sounds of bachata and salsa. Established producers such as DJ Playero, DJ Nelson, and DJ Joe, as well as newcomers like DJ Blass, helped move the genre's primary sound sources from samples to synthesizers resonant of trace-style techno anthems.

The rise and success of Luny tunes and the definitive role they played in shaping contemporary reggaetón symbolize the central role migration has played in the ongoing formation and reformation of the genre. Luny (Francisco Saldana) and Tunes (Victor Cabrera) were born in the Dominican Republic before moving to Massachusetts as teenagers. The duo set themselves apart from their forebearers by advancing a distinctive, pop-oriented melodic and harmonic language to reggaetón to embody a sonic form of the flashy blin-blineo/bling-bling style borrowed from commercial hip-hop.
In this new, commercialized version, the music was increasingly produced and promoted as the soundtrack of "

". Mirroring the commercialism and exaggerated sexual license associated with club culture, song themes turned more squarely to macho fantasies about sex. Reggaetón appeared to embrace, if not amplify, a number of racial, gendered, and national stereotypes as the music moved into the mainstream marketplace. Given such changes in context and content, the genre's cultural politics are often seen as undergoing a major shift around the turn of the millennium, moving away from a sonically, textually, and visually encoded foregrounding of racial community toward the urban sound of a pan-ethnic transnational "Latin" community. In fact, Luny Tunes proved crucial in moving the genre into the realm of "Latin" music by invoking distinctive salsa and merengue piano riffs and other nationalist and gendered Latin/pan-Latin signifiers.
